title: Isaiah 66
subtitle: All Nations, All Flesh: The Final Vision

sections.header: Verses 1–14 — “Heaven Is My Throne and the Earth Is My Footstool”

heb.paragraph: "Heaven is my throne (kissēʾ) and the earth is my footstool. Where is the house you will build me?" (v.1). Stephen quotes this in Acts 7:49–50 at his trial. God cannot be housed; temple-building is presumptu ous without the humility of v.2: "These are the ones I look on with favour: those who are humble and contrite in spirit, and who tremble at my word." The 57:15 paradox restated: the throne-God favours the trembling. Zion gives birth before labour pains — a nation born in a day (v.8). "As a mother comforts her child, so will I comfort you; and you will be comforted over Jerusalem" (v.13). God as MOTHER: the maternal comfort that answers ch.40’s opening "comfort, comfort." "You will see this and your heart will rejoice" (v.14).

notes.note: "Heaven is my throne, earth my footstool. Where is the house you will build me?" Acts 7:49–50: Stephen’s defence. The temple-critique: God inhabits heaven, not buildings. He looks on the humble and trembling.
"Before she goes into labour, she gives birth. Who has ever seen anything like this? Can a country be born in a day?" Birth without labour: the new creation is sudden, unprecedented, miraculous.
"As a mother comforts her child, so will I comfort you." God as mother. The book that opened with "comfort, comfort" (40:1) closes with maternal comfort. The inclusio is complete.

refs.note: Stephen quotes Isaiah to argue that God does not dwell in temples made by hands, challenging temple-centered religion.
Paul's Areopagus speech uses similar logic: the God who made the cosmos is not served by human hands.
Jesus's prohibition of swearing by heaven (God's throne) or earth (his footstool) echoes Isaiah's cosmic imagery.

hist.context: The challenge to temple-building ('what is the house you will build me?') is startling in a book that elsewhere celebrates Zion. The point is not anti-temple but anti-formalism: ritual without humility is rejected. The 'one who trembles at my word' becomes the true temple, anticipating later spiritualization of sacred space.

sections.header: Verses 15–24 — “All Flesh Will Come and Bow Down Before Me”

heb.paragraph: "From one New Moon to another and from one Sabbath to another, all flesh (kol bāsār) will come and bow down before me, says the LORD" (v.23). The final vision. God comes with fire and chariots to judge (v.15). Those who consecrate themselves for pagan practices will meet their end (v.17). "I am about to come and gather the people of all nations and languages, and they will come and see my glory" (v.18). Survivors are sent to distant nations — Tarshish, Libya, Lydia, Tubal, Greece, distant islands — "and they will proclaim my glory among the nations" (v.19). "They will bring all your people from all the nations as an offering to the LORD" (v.20). "I will select some of them also to be priests and Levites" (v.21) — GENTILES as priests. The new heavens and new earth endure (v.22). All flesh worships forever (v.23). The book closes with v.24: the dead bodies of the rebels, where "the worms that eat them do not die, and the fire that burns them is not quenched." Jesus quotes this three times in Mark 9:48 for Gehenna. Isaiah ends with both the universal worship of v.23 AND the eternal judgment of v.24. Both are the final word.

notes.note: "I am about to gather the people of all nations. They will come and see my glory. They will proclaim my glory among the nations." The global mission: every nation, every language. The nations who never heard will see.
"I will select some of them to be priests and Levites." Gentiles as Levitical priests. The most radical inclusion statement in the OT. The priesthood itself opens to all nations.

"The new heavens and the new earth that I make will endure before me. All flesh will come and bow down before me." The endurance promise: what God creates will last. The worship: universal, perpetual, all-encompassing.

"The worms do not die, and the fire is not quenched." Jesus quotes this in Mark 9:48. Isaiah’s last verse is not comfort but warning. The book refuses to end on only one note. Worship AND judgment. Grace AND fire. Both are Isaiah’s final word.

refs.note: Jesus quotes the undying worm and unquenching fire when warning about Gehenna.
The smoke of torment rising forever echoes Isaiah's perpetual judgment imagery.
Nations bringing glory into the holy city while nothing unclean enters develops Isaiah's pilgrimage vision.

hist.context: Isaiah concludes with all flesh worshipping before YHWH and viewing the corpses of rebels whose 'worm does not die and fire is not quenched.' This harsh juxtaposition of universal worship and eternal judgment became foundational for later Jewish and Christian eschatology. The final verse's graphic imagery was so disturbing that synagogue reading traditionally repeats verse 23 after verse 24 to end on a positive note.

ppl.name: nations
ppl.text: "I am about to gather the people of all nations…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHear the word of the Lord, you who tremble at his word: “Your own people who hate you, and exclude you because of my name, have said, ‘Let the Lord be glorified, that we may see your joy!’ Yet they will be put to shame.ESVHear the word of the LORD, you who tremble at his word: \u201cYour brothers who hate you and cast you out for my name\u2019s sake have said, \u2018Let the LORD be glorified, that we may see your joy\u2019; but it is they who shall be put to shame.NIVso I also will choose harsh treatment for them and will bring on them what they dread. For when I called, no one answered, when I spoke, no one listened. They did evil in my sight and chose what displeases me.”ESVI also will choose harsh treatment for them and bring their fears upon them, because when I called, no one answered, when I spoke, they did not listen; but they did what was evil in my eyes and chose that in which I did not delight.\u201d

rows.label: All Flesh Worships — 66:15–24
rows.text: "ALL FLESH will bow before me." Gentiles as PRIESTS. "Worms do not die, fire not quenched" (Mark 9:48). Both worship AND judgment.

hebtext.note: "Heaven is my throne (kissēʾ) and the earth is my footstool. Where is the house you will build me?" (v.1). Stephen quotes this in Acts 7:49–50 at his trial. God cannot be housed; temple-building is presumptu ous without the humility of v.2: "These are the ones I look on with favour: those who are humble and contrite in spirit, and who tremble at my word." "As a mother comforts her child, so will I comfort you; and you will be comforted over Jerusalem" (v.13). God as MOTHER: the maternal comfort that answers ch.40’s opening "comfort, comfort." "You will see this and your heart will rejoice" (v.14).

"From one New Moon to another and from one Sabbath to another, all flesh (kol bāsār) will come and bow down before me, says the LORD" (v.23). The final vision. God comes with fire and chariots to judge (v.15). Those who consecrate themselves for pagan practices will meet their end (v.17). "I am about to come and gather the people of all nations and languages, and they will come and see my glory" (v.18). Survivors are sent to distant nations — Tarshish, Libya, Lydia, Tubal, Greece, distant islands — "and they will proclaim my glory among the nations" (v.19). "They will bring all your people from all the nations as an offering to the LORD" (v.20). "I will select some of them also to be priests and Levites" (v.21) — GENTILES as priests. The new heavens and new earth endure (v.22). All flesh worships forever (v.23). The book closes with v.24: the dead bodies of the rebels, where "the worms that eat them do not die, and the fire that burns them is not quenched." Jesus quotes this three times in Mark 9:48 for Gehenna. Isaiah ends with both the universal worship of v.23 AND the eternal judgment of v.24. Both are the final word.

themes.note: Mission peaks at 10: all nations gathered, all flesh worships, Gentiles as priests. Worship and Sovereignty at 9: the final vision. Prophecy at 9: Mark 9:48, Acts 7:49. Isaiah closes with both universal worship AND eternal judgment.
